segments.paradiso-6-g25: Tosto che is a temporal conjunction meaning “as soon as.” Con la Chiesa mossi i piedi is literal word order: mossi is the first-person passato remoto of a poetic verb 'mossare' (“I moved/set in motion”), and i piedi (lit. “the feet”) forms the idiom 'movere i piedi' = 'to set out/leave/march'; so the whole clause reads “as soon as I set out with the Church.”
segments.paradiso-6-g26: Piacque is third-person passato remoto of piacere in an impersonal/psychological construction: 'it pleased (God)…' with a datival a Dio. Per grazia = 'by/through grace' (circumstantial). Di spirarmi is 'to breathe/inhale/instill into me' where spirarmi is the infinitive with enclitic object mi; l’alto lavoro is the direct object of spirare: 'to inspire in me the lofty task.'
segments.paradiso-6-g27: E tutto ’n lui mi diedi is idiomatic: e 'and'; tutto = 'completely/everything' (adverbial force here); ’n is the contracted form of in (meaning 'into' or 'to' in older orthography) and lui refers back to l'alto lavoro ('to it'); mi diedi is passato remoto first person of darsi (to give oneself) = 'I gave myself' or 'I devoted myself.'
segments.paradiso-6-g28: E introduces the next action; al = a + il 'to the'; mio Belisar is the recipient ('my Belisarius'); commendai is first-person passato remoto of commendare (here 'I entrusted/committed'); l’armi (the article elided before a vowel) is the plural direct object 'the arms'.
segments.paradiso-6-g29: Cui introduces a relative clause referring to Belisar and functions as 'to whom' here. La destra del ciel is a genitive phrase 'the right hand of heaven' (del = di + il). Fu is passato remoto 'was' and congiunta is the past participle agreeing with destra: 'was so joined.' Sì intensifies congiunta: 'so joined.'
segments.paradiso-6-g30: Che here introduces a result/explanatory clause: 'that'; segno fu = 'it was a sign' (segno subject, fu = was); ch’ = that (introducing subordinate clause); i (here an archaic form of 'io' or a typo in the apparatus—this word as given does not match the indicated senses) would be the subject 'I' in 'ch’i’ dovessi' but is orthographically contracted; dovessi is the imperfect subjunctive of dover (modal 'ought/should' in an oblique result clause), and posarmi is the infinitive with enclitic me 'to lay myself down/to rest' — meaning 'that I ought to rest/lay down (the arms).'
segments.paradiso-6-g31: Or qui = 'now here/at this point.' A la question prima = 'to the first question' (a + la). S’appunta is a pronominal/personal present form of appuntare (si appunta) meaning 'is directed/aimed at' or 'applies to'; la mia risposta is the subject/complement: 'my answer (is directed to)'.
segments.paradiso-6-g32: Ma introduces contrast: 'but.' Sua condizione = 'its condition/circumstances' (sua refers back to the question or preceding matter). Mi stringe uses stringe (present indicative) with indirect object mi = 'it presses/compels me'; a seguitare = 'to follow/continue' (infinitive); alcuna giunta = 'some addition/brief supplement' (alcuna used adverbially/nounally = 'some/a certain').
segments.paradiso-6-g33: Perché here = 'so that' (introducing purpose/result): 'so that you may see.' Tu veggi is a present subjunctive or jussive form meaning 'you may/might see'; con quanta ragione = 'with how much reason/with what justification'; si move is an impersonal/reflexive construction 'one moves/people act' (si + move); contr’ al sacrosanto segno = 'against the sacrosanct sign/standard' (contr’ is contracted contra); e chi ’l s’appropria e chi a lui s’oppone lists 'who claims/appropriates it and who opposes it' — l' is the clitic for 'lo' (it), s’appropria = 'takes/claims for oneself,' s’oppone = 'opposes.'
segments.paradiso-6-g34: Vedi is 2nd-person singular present (literally “you see”); Dante often uses it to call the listener’s attention. Quanta virtù is an exclamatory noun phrase (“how much/what a power/virtue”) modifying the verb phrase that follows. L’ha fatto is the auxiliary ha + past participle fatto with the clitic l’ (lo) attached to the auxiliary: l’ha fatto = “has made him”; degno di reverenza is a predicative adjective with the preposition di introducing the thing one is worthy of (“worthy of reverence”).
segments.paradiso-6-g35: E cominciò: e = “and”; cominciò is passato remoto, 3rd-person singular, “he/it began.” Da l’ora = da + the definite article l’ (here the article before a vowel), meaning “from the hour/time.” Che introduces a temporal subordinate clause (here: “when/that”); Pallante morì is a simple past (morì, passato remoto) “Pallante died.” Per + infinitive (per darli) expresses purpose or result here (“in order to give”); darli is the infinitive dare with the enclitic pronoun -li attached (“to give him” in older/clitic usage), and regno is the direct object “kingdom.”
segments.paradiso-6-g36: Tu sai is present indicative, 2nd-person singular (“you know”). Ch’ is the apocopated form of che introducing the subordinate clause. El is an archaic subject pronoun (= he) used by Dante instead of modern egli/lui. Fece is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of fare here meaning “he made” or “he established” (i.e. ‘took up residence’). In Alba sua dimora = in + the place name Alba (proper name) + possessive + dimora (“his dwelling/stay”). Per trecento anni e oltre uses per to mark duration (“for three hundred years and more”), with oltre adding “beyond/more.”
segments.paradiso-6-g37: Infino al fine che is a temporal phrase meaning “until the end/unto the time that,” with al = a + il. Che introduces the clause that follows. I tre = “the three” (definite article + numeral). A’ tre (a + tre, here written a’ for metrical reasons) means “three by three / three against three” (groups of three); pugnar appears in the infinitive form but functions as a poetic/historical infinitive used as a narrative verb meaning “they fought.” Per lui ancora: per = “for/on behalf of,” lui = “him,” ancora = “still/still again.”
segments.paradiso-6-g62: Two names joined by the coordinating conjunction e; the proper nouns are the direct objects of the verb in the clause that follows. The conjunction e simply links the two place/person names as a single list.
segments.paradiso-6-g63: Onde is a relative/adverbial 'from which' indicating origin; si mosse is reflexive passato remoto (si + mosse) meaning 'he moved/set out (himself)'. The reflexive si makes the action intransitive (he departed); mosse here is past tense referring back to the subject of the surrounding verse.
segments.paradiso-6-g64: Rivide is passato remoto 'he saw again' (re- + vide). E links this clause to the previous; là 'there' and dov' (elided dove) 'where' introduce a subordinate locative clause. Ettore is the subject of the embedded clause and si cuba uses the reflexive pronoun with the archaic verb cuba (from Latin cubare) meaning 'lies' or 'is laid down' — an older poetic verb meaning 'to lie (down)'.
segments.paradiso-6-g65: E 'and' continues the narrative; mal here is an adverb 'badly' or 'to ill effect'. Per is a preposition meaning 'by/through/because of' and introduces the agent Tolomeo (Ptolemy). Poscia is an archaic adverb 'afterwards'; si scosse is reflexive passato remoto of scuotere/scossare meaning 'he was shaken' or 'he shook himself' (figuratively 'was disturbed').
segments.paradiso-6-g66: Da 'from' marks origin; indi (here 'thence') is a poetical adverb meaning 'from there' (note the manuscript spelling); scese is passato remoto of scendere 'he descended'. Folgorando is a present participle/gerund used adverbially 'lightning-striking' or 'lightning-like' describing the manner of descent. A introduces the proper name Iuba (Juba) as the destination.
segments.paradiso-6-g67: Onde 'from which/thence' links to the previous clause; si volse is reflexive passato remoto of volgere, 'he turned himself' or 'he turned toward'. Nel (in + il) introduces vostro occidente 'your west', with vostro as the possessive addressing the listener/people invoked.
segments.paradiso-6-g68: Ove is a poetic form of dove, 'where'; sentia is the imperfect tense (sentiva in modern Italian) of sentire used here to mean 'he heard' or 'he perceived'. La Pompeana tuba is a noun phrase: la + pompeana (an adjective referring to Pompey/Pompeian or pomp) + tuba 'trumpet'. The imperfect indicates a continuing or habitual perception in the scene.
segments.paradiso-6-g69: Di quel che = 'concerning that which' (di + relative clause). Fé is an archaic spelling of fece, passato remoto 'he did/made'. Col (con + il) baiulo seguente means 'with the following bearer/carrier' — baiulo is an agent/noun; seguente is an adjective 'following' or 'next'.
segments.paradiso-6-g70: Bruto (Brutus) is the subject; con Cassio 'with Cassius' is an accompaniment. Ne l' inferno is the older split of nella inferno 'in the Hell' (ne + l' = nella). Latra is present indicative 'he/it barks' used figuratively of Brutus barking in Hell.
segments.paradiso-6-g71: E coordinates the final clause; Modena e Perugia are two cities joined by e. Fu is passato remoto of essere 'were/was' and dolente is a predicate adjective 'sorrowful' agreeing with the compound subject (the two cities are said to have been sorrowful).
segments.paradiso-6-g72: Piangene is piangere + enclitic ne and here functions as a present/pluperfect/imperfective form meaning 'they mourn her' or 'mourn her' with ne referring to 'her' (Cleopatra). Ancor is 'still/yet'; la trista Cleopatra is the direct object phrase 'the sad Cleopatra'. The verb form and clitic are idiomatic and somewhat archaic/poetic.
segments.paradiso-6-g73: Che is the relative pronoun 'who/which/that' introducing the subordinate clause that describes Cleopatra; it refers back to Cleopatra and links the next participial clause to her.
segments.paradiso-6-g74: Fuggendoli is the gerund/present participle of fuggere with the clitic -li (them) attached: 'fleeing them' or 'fleeing from them'. Innanzi is an adverb 'ahead' or 'before' indicating direction (she fled ahead of them).
segments.paradiso-6-g75: Dal (da + il) introduces the agent/source 'from the' or 'by the'; colubro is 'snake' (the asp). La morte 'the death' is the direct object; prese is passato remoto of prendere 'took' or 'seized' (here 'met with' or 'fell to'). Subitana is an adjective 'sudden' and atra is an adjective 'black/gloomy' — both modify morte. Word order is typical poetic placement with the agent first (dal colubro) and the object and its modifiers following.
segments.paradiso-6-g51: Poi is a temporal adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards' introducing the next sequence of events; placed at the start of the verse it marks a shift forward in time.
segments.paradiso-6-g52: 'presso al tempo che' is an idiom meaning 'about/near the time when'; presso here = 'near' + al tempo = 'the time'. 'che' introduces a subordinate clause. 'tutto 'l ciel' = 'all the heaven' (tutto + the omitted vowel in the article 'l' = il). 'volle' is a past, literary/archaic form meaning 'willed' or 'desired' (past tense of volere). 'redur' is an archaic infinitive (modern ridurre or rendere) meaning 'to restore/bring back'; 'lo mondo' (the world) is the direct object, and 'a suo modo sereno' means 'to its calm/serene manner/state' (suo = its).
segments.paradiso-6-g53: Main clause: 'Cesare' is the subject. 'per voler di Roma' means 'by (the) will of Rome' where 'per' here is instrumental ('by/through') and 'voler' is used as a noun meaning 'will/pleasure'. 'il' is the object pronoun (here written as an unstressed il = lo, 'him/it') and 'tolle' is an archaic verb form from Latin tollere meaning 'take away/remove' (here past sense 'took away'). The whole says Caesar, at Rome's bidding, took him away.
segments.paradiso-6-g54: E = 'and'. 'quel che fé' = 'that which he did / what he accomplished' ('fé' = fece, past tense of fare). 'da Varo infino a Reno' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from the Var up to the Rhine' (da ... infino a = 'from ... as far as').
segments.paradiso-6-g55: Series of verbs with repeated subject: 'Isara vide ed Era e vide Senna' = 'he saw the Isère and the Aare and saw the Seine.' 'ed' is the form of 'e' used before a vowel. 'ogne valle' = 'every valley' (ogni spelled ogne in older orthography). 'onde' = 'from which' (relative adverb). 'Rodano è pieno' = 'the Rhône is full' (è = present of essere; pieno = full).
segments.paradiso-6-g56: 'Quel che fé poi' = 'what he then did/what followed'. 'ch’elli uscì di Ravenna' = 'when he left Ravenna' (ch’ = che; elli = he; uscì = left). 'e saltò Rubicon' = 'and crossed/jumped the Rubicon' (saltò = jumped/passed over), all describing the subsequent action.
segments.paradiso-6-g57: 'fu' is the past tense of essere ('was'). 'di tal volo' literally 'of such a flight/sweep' is a metaphor meaning 'of such swift, far-reaching action' — volo here = 'flight' or 'rapid campaign'.
segments.paradiso-6-g58: Result clause introduced by 'che' = 'that/so that'. 'nol' is the contraction of 'non lo' = 'not it/him' (i.e., 'it could not'). 'seguiteria' here functions as an archaic verbal form meaning 'would follow' or 'could follow' (the form is literary/poetic). 'lingua né penna' = 'neither tongue nor pen' — idiomatically 'neither speech nor writing could follow/describe it.'
segments.paradiso-6-g59: 'Inver’ la Spagna' = 'toward Spain' (inver' = in verso / verso = toward). 'rivolse' is the past of rivolgere meaning 'turned/directed'. 'lo stuolo' = 'the host/army' (direct object). The clause says he directed his army toward Spain.
segments.paradiso-6-g60: Short temporal/directional phrase: 'poi' = 'then/afterwards'; 'ver’ Durazzo' = 'toward Durazzo' (ver' is a poetic contraction of verso/verso a). The phrase lists the next movement of the army.
segments.paradiso-6-g61: Compound clause: 'e Farsalia percosse' = 'and he struck Farsalia / struck at Pharsalus' (percosse = struck/attacked). 'sì ch’' = 'so that'. 'al Nil caldo si sentì del duolo' = 'the warm Nile itself felt the grief' (al = to the/the; Nil = Nile; caldo = warm; si sentì = 'felt itself' / 'felt'; del duolo = 'of the grief'). This is a poetic hyperbole: even the Nile felt sorrow.
segments.paradiso-6-g38: E is the simple coordinating conjunction “and.” sai is 2nd person singular present indicative of sapere (“you know”). ch’ is the clitic form of che introducing a subordinate clause (“that”). el is an archaic subject pronoun meaning “he.” fé is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd singular of fare, meaning “did/made.” dal is the contraction da + il “from the/the,” and mal here means a wrong or harm (not physical illness). de le Sabine is the prepositional phrase “of the Sabines/Sabine women” (le = feminine plural article). al dolor di Lucrezia forms a parallel noun phrase introduced by al (a + il) meaning “to the sorrow of Lucretia,” so the clause sets up a range “from X to Y.” in sette regi is a prepositional phrase with in + numeral giving the sphere or scope (“in/under seven kings”); regi’s sense is not supplied in the given lexicon, so it is left unspecified.
segments.paradiso-6-g39: vincendo is the present participle / gerund of vincere and gives a concomitant or causal idea here (“by/while defeating” or simply “defeating”). intorno is an adverb meaning “around/all about.” le genti vicine is the direct object: le (feminine plural article) + genti (peoples/peoples groups; plural of gente used poetically) + vicine (adjective agreeing in number and placed after the noun: “neighbouring/nearby”).
segments.paradiso-6-g40: Sai is again 2nd singular present “you know.” quel ch’ is “that which/what” (quel + clitic che) introducing the object clause. el is the subject pronoun “he.” fé is passato remoto 3rd sing of fare (“did/achieved”). portato is the past participle of portare used here as a complement to fé (literal “did, carried/brought”), and da + li egregi Romani is an agent phrase with da = “by” and li egregi Romani = “the distinguished Romans.” incontro a + name means “to meet/face/against” that person (incontro used as “encounter/face”).
segments.paradiso-6-g41: A repeated short phrase built like the previous: incontro a Pirro = “to encounter/against Pyrrhus,” with incontro used as a verb/lexical noun meaning “meet/face.” This is an elliptical repetition for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-6-g42: Again rencontre formula: incontro a = “against/to meet”; li altri principi e collegi = “the other princes and collegia/assemblies.” Note that principe here means “prince” (ruler) though the provided lexical senses for principi do not record that meaning, so the precise sense index is not available in the given list.
segments.paradiso-6-g43: onde is a relative/adverb meaning “whence/wherefrom/therefore,” introducing the consequence or source. Torquato e Quinzio are proper names (Torquatus and Quinctius). The phrase signals that what follows explains the fame these men had.
segments.paradiso-6-g44: che introduces a relative clause qualifying Quinzio: dal cirro negletto is a prepositional phrase (da + il) meaning “from/by the neglected cirrus/lock” — cirro here is a poetic word (literally a lock or thin cloud) and negletto is the past participle/adjective “neglected/overlooked.” fu nomato is a passive-like construction with fu (passato remoto of essere, “was”) + nomato (past participle of nominare) meaning “was named/called.”
segments.paradiso-6-g45: i Deci e ’ Fabi is a nominative list of families (the Decii and the Fabii) preceded by the definite plural article i. ebber is passato remoto 3rd plural of avere (“they had/received”), and la fama is the direct object “the fame/reputation.” che volontier mirro is a subordinate clause introduced by che: volontier is an adverb “gladly,” and mirro here is a verbal or verbal-like term meaning something like “I would behold/admire” in the poet’s usage; the exact lexical sense given in the supplied list does not match this verbal use and so is left unspecified.
segments.paradiso-6-g46: Esso is the neuter/third-person pronoun “it.” atterrò is passato remoto 3rd singular of atterrare used transitively here, meaning “knocked down/overturned/humbled.” l’ orgoglio de li Aràbi is the object phrase: the article + noun + de (archaic form of di) + li (plural article) + Aràbi (Arabs). che introduces a relative clause; di retro ad Anibale is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the wake of/behind Hannibal” (di retro = “after/behind”), and passaro is an archaic past plural form of passare (they crossed/passed), here agreeing with Aràbi as its subject. l’ alpestre rocce is the object of passaro: “the alpine rocks.” Note that passaro is presented in the supplied lexicon as a noun but here functions as a verb (hence no matching sense index).
segments.paradiso-6-g47: Po is an appositive naming the river (the Po) placed parenthetically and marked off by the comma; it specifies the ‘alpine rocks’/river referred to in the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-6-g48: di che is a relative phrase meaning “of which/about which.” tu is the 2nd person singular subject “you.” labi is a present form of labare (an archaic/poetic verb) meaning roughly “you speak of/you mention” or more literally “you waver/totter” in the supplied senses; here it functions as the verb of the relative clause referring back to Po.
segments.paradiso-6-g49: Sott’ (sotto elided before a vowel) is the preposition “under/beneath.” esso is the pronoun “it.” giovanetti here refers to young men (a noun usage rather than the verbal senses given in the supplied lexicon), and trïunfaro is a poetic/archaic form meaning “triumphed/triumphal” (presenting the action/result of triumph). Scipïone e Pompeo are the proper names given as examples of those young men who triumphed.
segments.paradiso-6-g50: e is “and.” a quel colle is a prepositional phrase “at/to that hill” (a + quel + colle). sotto ’l qual = “under which” (sotto + the relative pronoun qual referring to colle). tu nascesti uses nascesti, passato remoto 2nd singular of nascere, “you were born.” parve is passato remoto 3rd singular of parere (“it seemed/appeared”), and amaro is the predicate adjective “bitter.” Together the clause says that the hill beneath which you were born seemed bitter (figuratively painful) to someone/contextually relevant.
segments.paradiso-6-g1: "Poscia che" is a temporal conjunction meaning "after / when." Grammatically, Costantin is the subject, l'aquila the direct object (the imperial eagle), and volse is a past-tense verb (volse = volgere, "turned") describing the action; contr' al corso del ciel is a contracted prepositional phrase (contra → contr') marking direction toward "the course of heaven."
segments.paradiso-6-g2: Here ch' = che introduces a relative clause "which/that." Ella refers back to the eagle. Seguìo is the passato remoto (seguire) "followed"; dietro a = "behind / after" and l'antico = "the ancient one/ancestor." The clause closes with che Lavina tolse: a defining relative clause where tolse (passato remoto of togliere) means "took."
segments.paradiso-6-g3: This is a temporal/quantitative phrase: "cento e cent' anni e più" = "a hundred and a hundred years and more." L'uccel di Dio = "the bird of God." Ne lo stremo d'Europa si ritenne uses the reflexive si with ritenne (passato remoto of ritenere) meaning "kept/held itself" or "remained" in the extremity of Europe (ne lo stremo = in the far end).
segments.paradiso-6-g4: Vicino a' monti = "near the mountains." De' quai prima uscìo: de' (di) + quai (archaic/poetic form referring back to those mountains, "from which / where") and prima uscìo (passato remoto uscire) "first issued/emerged." The clause states location and origin: near the mountains from which it first came out.
segments.paradiso-6-g5: E sotto l'ombra de le sacre penne = "and under the shade / protection of the sacred feathers (wings)." Governò is passato remoto "it governed/ruled," ’l mondo is the direct object, lì "there," and di mano in mano is an idiom meaning "from hand to hand" (i.e. from ruler to ruler).
segments.paradiso-6-g6: A simple coordinating conjunction "e" linking the previous clause to the next; it carries the sense "and" or "and then."
segments.paradiso-6-g7: Sì cangiando is a circumstantial gerund phrase: sì = "thus / in that way," cangiando is the gerund of cangiare (to change), meaning "thus changing" and modifying the subject of the previous clause (the eagle).
segments.paradiso-6-g8: In su = "upon / onto," la mia is elliptical ("mine" or "my [domain/standard]") and pervenne is passato remoto of pervenire, "arrived / reached." Grammatically pervenne takes the prepositional phrase in su + la mia as its complement: "it came upon mine."
segments.paradiso-6-g9: A pair of simple copular statements: Cesare fui = "I was Caesar" (fui = passato remoto of essere), and son Iustinïano = "I am Justinian" (son = present tense). Dante uses past and present together to state former and current identity.
segments.paradiso-6-g10: Single relative pronoun che introducing the following explanatory subordinate clause ("who/which").
segments.paradiso-6-g11: Per + noun voler expresses motive or means: "by the will / through the desire of the first love that I feel." Del primo amor = "of the first love," and ch'i' sento is the relative clause "that I feel" (ch' = che; i' = io contracted). Sento is present tense, indicating an abiding feeling.
segments.paradiso-6-g12: D'entro le leggi = "from within the laws" (d'entro = da dentro). Trassi is first person passato remoto (trarre) "I drew / I extracted." Il troppo e 'l vano are abstract nouns: "the excess and the vain (things)." The clause states what he derived from the laws.
segments.paradiso-6-g13: Prima che + subjunctive: "Before I should be / before I was" — ch'io fossi attento uses the imperfect subjunctive fossi after prima che to mark an action not yet realized at that past time ("before I turned my attention to the task").
segments.paradiso-6-g14: Una natura in Cristo esser is an object/clause expressing Dante's past belief: "that there is one nature in Christ." Esser is the bare infinitive "to be," used as the copula of the content clause.
segments.paradiso-6-g15: Non piùe is an archaic negative phrase meaning "no more / no other." Here it qualifies the previous clause (i.e. he believed there was no more than one nature).
segments.paradiso-6-g16: Credea is the imperfect indicative "I believed" stating Dante's past belief; it governs the preceding content clause.
segments.paradiso-6-g17: E di tal fede era contento: era is imperfect "was" and contento an adjective meaning "content/pleased with such faith." This describes his past state of satisfaction with that belief.
segments.paradiso-6-g18: Contrastive but: ma introduces the corrective clause; l' benedetto Agapito = "the blessed Agapitus" (article + epithet + proper name).
segments.paradiso-6-g19: Che fue sommo pastore is a relative clause identifying Agapito: che = who; fue = passato remoto of essere ("was"); sommo pastore = "supreme shepherd" (i.e. the highest pastor/pope).
segments.paradiso-6-g20: A la fede sincera mi dirizzò: dirizzò (passato remoto) is transitive here meaning "directed / guided me," with indirect object mi and the phrase a la fede sincera indicating the goal/direction. Con le parole sue "with his words" indicates the means by which he guided Dante.
segments.paradiso-6-g21: Io li credetti: Io = subject "I," li is an object clitic "him/it" (archaic form), and credetti is passato remoto "I believed / I gave credence to him." The clause is a short, emphatic past-statement.
segments.paradiso-6-g22: E ciò che 'n sua fede era = "and that which was in his faith." Ciò che introduces a content clause; 'n is the contraction of in; sua fede is the possession; era is the imperfect "was."
segments.paradiso-6-g23: Vegg' io or chiaro sì: vegg' is an archaic present-tense form of "I see" (veggo/veggio), with the enclitic subject order (vegg' io). Or = "now," chiaro = "clearly," and sì = "so / thus." The whole phrase = "I now see it so clearly."
segments.paradiso-6-g24: Come tu vedi = "as you see." Ogni contradizione e falsa e vera: ogni = "every," contradizione = "contradiction," and the coordinate adjectives e falsa e vera mean "both false and true," emphasizing that contradictions of both kinds are now visible.
segments.paradiso-6-g76: Con costui = 'with this one / with him' (preposition + demonstrative). 'corse' is the passato remoto (or poetic past) of correre used here as an intransitive past tense 'ran' or 'rushed'; the subject is the thing spoken of earlier and is elliptical. 'infino al lito rubro' is an adverbial phrase 'as far as / up to the red shore' where 'infino (a)' = 'up to', 'al' = preposition + article, 'lito rubro' = 'red shore' (adjectival postposition typical of poetry).
segments.paradiso-6-g77: 'con costui' again 'with him'. 'puose' is the passato remoto of porre (poetic form 'pòrre' → 'pose/put') meaning 'placed/put'. The construction 'con costui puose il mondo in tanta pace' means 'he/it set the world into so great a peace' (subject again understood from context). 'in tanta pace' is the result-state: 'into such peace'.
segments.paradiso-6-g78: 'che' introduces a result clause 'so that / so... that'. 'fu serrato' is a passive/perfect sense: 'was shut/closed' (fu = past auxiliary essere, serrato = past participle of serrare). 'a Giano' = 'to/at Janus' (meaning 'at the temple of Janus'); 'il suo delubro' = 'his shrine/temple' (literally 'his temple', delubro = shrine). Word order is canonical for result: result clause follows 'che'.
segments.paradiso-6-g79: This long clause folds several relatives and tenses. 'Ma' = 'but'. 'ciò che' = 'that which'. '’l segno che parlar mi face' = 'the sign that makes me speak' (relative clause: 'che' + verb 'face' from fare, present-type meaning 'causes'). 'fatto avea prima' = pluperfect ('avea' + past participle 'fatto') = 'had done/made before'. 'e poi era fatturo' is an archaic/poetic periphrasis meaning 'and afterwards had been brought about/completed' ('fatturo' is an irregular/poetic form of a past participle). 'per lo regno mortal ch’a lui soggiace' = 'for the mortal kingdom that is subject to him' (relative 'ch’' = che, 'soggiace' = present 'lies under / is subject to').
segments.paradiso-6-g80: 'diventa' (present tense) here means 'becomes' or 'appears as'. 'in apparenza' = 'in appearance / apparently'. 'poco e scuro' are predicative adjectives: 'slight/insignificant and obscure' (poco = slight, scuro = obscure here). This clause says the thing appears small and obscure when considered in that way.
segments.paradiso-6-g81: 'se' = 'if'. 'in mano a' = 'in the hand/power of' (idiom meaning 'in the hands of'). 'terzo Cesare' = 'the third Caesar' (a title); 'si mira' is an impersonal/reflexive passive 'is seen / is beheld'. 'con occhio chiaro e con affetto puro' are two adverbial phrases 'with a clear eye and with pure feeling', specifying the manner in which it should be regarded. Word order places the condition first, then the manner.
segments.paradiso-6-g82: 'ché' = 'for/because'. 'la viva giustizia' = 'the living (active) justice' (adjective after article). 'che mi spira' = relative 'that breathes/emanates to me' or 'that inspires me' — 'spira' present indicative from spirare used metaphorically 'to breathe/instill'. The clause explains the agency behind the granting mentioned next.
segments.paradiso-6-g83: 'li' is an enclitic pronoun referring back to those matters (here 'them' = the powers/actions previously described). 'concedette' is passato remoto of concedere, 'granted' or 'allowed'. Together: 'granted them.'
segments.paradiso-6-g84: 'in mano a' = 'into the hand/power of'. 'quel ch’i’ dico' = 'that one whom I speak of' (relative 'ch’' = che, 'i’' = io archaic enclitic, 'dico' = 'I say / I mean'). The phrase specifies to whom the granting was made.
segments.paradiso-6-g85: 'gloria di far vendetta' = 'the glory / honour of doing/bringing about vengeance' — 'di far' is the infinitive clause 'to do/inflict'. 'a la sua ira' = 'against / on his wrath' or 'for his anger' (prepositional phrase indicating the target or reason). The nominal phrase expresses the honour granted: the right/glory to exact vengeance.
segments.paradiso-6-g86: 'Or' = 'now'. 'qui' = 'here / at this point'. 't’ammira' = verb ammira with enclitic 'ti' (t’) — literally 'it amazes you' or 'you should marvel'; in this context the sense is imperative/evocative: 'here marvel (be amazed)'. 'in ciò ch’io ti replìco' = 'at that which I reply to you' (relative clause with 'ch’io' = 'that I').
segments.paradiso-6-g87: 'poscia' = 'afterwards/then'. 'con Tito' = 'with Titus'. 'a far vendetta' = 'to carry out / to take vengeance' (infinitive with preposition 'a' indicating purpose). 'corse' = passato remoto of correre, 'he ran / hurried' — used here as 'he rushed to do something'. 'de la vendetta del peccato antico' = 'for/of the vengeance corresponding to the ancient sin' (the phrase 'de la vendetta' marks the cause/subject of the action).
segments.paradiso-6-g88: 'E quando' = 'And when'. 'il dente longobardo' is a metaphorical phrase 'the Lombard tooth' (Lombard aggression or 'bite' of the Lombards). 'morse' is passato remoto of mordere = 'bit'. 'la Santa Chiesa' = direct object 'the Holy Church' (the Church as victim of that bite).
segments.paradiso-6-g89: 'sotto le sue ali' = 'under her wings' (idiom meaning under her protection). 'Carlo Magno' follows in apposition: 'Charles the Great' (Charlemagne). Word order places the protective image before the name.
segments.paradiso-6-g90: 'vincendo' is a present participle (vincere) 'overcoming / conquering' used adverbially to indicate the manner or circumstance ('having won / in victory'). It modifies Carlo Magno, giving the means or condition in which he intervened.
segments.paradiso-6-g91: 'la' = direct object pronoun referring to 'la Santa Chiesa' (her/it). 'soccorse' is passato remoto of soccorrere = 'he helped / came to the aid of'. Together 'la soccorse' = 'he came to her aid' or 'he helped her'.
segments.paradiso-6-g114: E = conjunction 'and'. 'Dentro a la presente margarita' is a locative prepositional phrase ('within/inside this pearl') in which 'dentro a' is a poetic locative construction followed by the article + adjective + noun. The first 'luce' is a verb (3rd person singular, present indicative 'shines'), and immediately after comes the noun 'la luce' ('the light') — a deliberate repetition of the same form (verb + noun) common in Dante's style, so the clause literally reads 'within this pearl shines the light...'.
segments.paradiso-6-g115: 'di cui' = 'of whom/whose', a relative phrase referring back to Romeo. 'Fu' is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of essere 'was'. 'L’ovra' (the article elided as l') is 'the work' (ovra = opera/work). The adjectives 'grande e bella' follow and qualify 'ovra'. 'Mal gradita' is an adjectival phrase: 'mal' (adverb 'badly') + 'gradita' (past participle used adjectivally = 'welcome/pleasing'), giving 'was ill-received' or 'was not welcomed'.
segments.paradiso-6-g116: 'Ma' = 'but'. 'I Provenzai' is the plural subject 'the Provençals' (people from Provence). The relative clause 'che fecer contra lui' uses the archaic past form 'fecer' (3rd pers. pl., medieval form of 'fecero/fecêr') meaning 'they did/acted' and 'contra lui' = 'against him'. 'Non hanno riso' is present perfect (avere + past participle riso) literally 'have not laughed' — here indicating they did not find it amusing or did not laugh.
segments.paradiso-6-g117: 'E però' links the consequence ('and therefore'). 'Mal cammina' is an idiom (literally 'walks badly') meaning ' fares poorly' or 'goes ill'. 'Qual' is a relative pronoun here ('who/one who'), 'si fa danno' uses the reflexive 'si' + 'fa' (present 'makes for himself/does to himself') and 'danno' = 'harm/damage'. 'Del ben fare altrui' = 'to the good doing of others' = 'another's good deeds'. The whole clause means 'and therefore ill fares he who does harm to another's good deeds.'
segments.paradiso-6-g118: Inverted word order: 'Quattro figlie ebbe' places the object before the verb; 'ebbe' is passato remoto (3rd person singular of avere) meaning 'he had'. The subject (Ramondo) appears later. So the clause states 'he had four daughters.'
segments.paradiso-6-g119: 'E ciascuna reina' is an elliptical nominative/predicative phrase: 'and each [one] (was) queen.' The verb 'was' is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis) and 'reina' functions as a predicative noun ('queen' / 'one who reigns').
segments.paradiso-6-g120: Apposition: 'Ramondo Beringhiere' names the subject introduced earlier by the verb 'ebbe' (the man who had the daughters). The proper name appears after the predicate because of Dante's word-order flexibility.
segments.paradiso-6-g121: 'E ciò li fece Romeo' = 'and Romeo made that (to them).' 'Ciò' = 'that/this' (the fact just stated), 'li' is an unstressed clitic pronoun referring to the daughters (object/indirect object clitic placed before the verb), and 'fece' is passato remoto of 'fare' (3rd pers. sing. 'made/did'); the subject 'Romeo' follows the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-6-g122: Appositive descriptive phrase modifying 'Romeo'. 'Persona' = 'person', followed by the adjectives 'umìle' (humble) and 'peregrina'. In Dante 'peregrina' often means 'pilgrim' or 'traveller', so the phrase characterises Romeo as 'a humble and pilgrim-like person.' Adjectives follow the noun, as is common in Italian.
segments.paradiso-6-g123: E poi = connective 'and then'. 'mosser' is an archaic past-form of the verb 'mosse/mossero' (here used as a verb meaning 'set in motion' or 'uttered'); it governs the following direct object. 'le parole biece' is a feminine plural noun phrase ('le parole' = 'the words', modified by 'biece' = 'harsh/wicked'). 'a dimandar ragione' is an infinitive purpose clause 'to demand an explanation' and 'a questo giusto' marks the person from whom the explanation is demanded ('from this just man').
segments.paradiso-6-g124: 'che' is a relative pronoun linking this clause to the previous action ('who/which/that'). 'li' is the clitic object 'them' (those persons or portions referred to). 'assegnò' is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of 'assegnare' = 'assigned' or 'allotted'. 'sette e cinque per diece' is a numerical phrase meaning literally 'seven and five for ten' (an idiomatic way to indicate the numbers given, i.e. seven and five making ten).
segments.paradiso-6-g125: 'indi' is an adverb meaning 'thence/then' (archaic narrative marker). 'partissi' is an archaic past form meaning 'he departed/left' (narrative past); it takes the rest of the clause as predicate complement. 'povero e vetusto' are predicate adjectives 'poor and old' (vetusto = old/venerable).
segments.paradiso-6-g126: This is a conditional clause: 'e se ... sapesse' = 'and if ... knew' with 'sapesse' the imperfect subjunctive expressing a hypothetical or counterfactual condition. '’l mondo' = 'the world' (people at large). 'il cor ch’elli ebbe' = 'the heart that he had' (ch' = contraction of 'che' = 'that/which', 'elli ebbe' = archaic 'he had'). 'mendicando' is a gerund ('by begging'), indicating the means; 'sua vita a frusto a frusto' is an idiomatic repetitive phrase 'his life bit by bit' (a frusto a frusto = little by little / morsel by morsel), describing the slow way he begged his living.
segments.paradiso-6-g127: 'assai' is an adverb meaning 'very/much'. 'lo loda' = 'praises him' (lo = direct object 'him'; 'loda' is present or historic-present indicative of 'lodare' = 'praise'). The clause states a general result/assessment: people praise him greatly.
segments.paradiso-6-g128: 'e più' = 'and even more'. 'loderebbe' is the conditional present of 'lodare' = 'would praise', expressing the hypothetical result if the earlier conditional were true; 'lo' again is the direct object 'him'. The sentence closes with this hypothetical increase in praise.
segments.paradiso-6-g92: Omai is an adverb meaning 'by now'. puoi is 2nd-person singular present indicative of potere ('you can'); it governs the following infinitive giudicar (poetic truncation of giudicare). The preposition di introduces the object of giudicar: quei cotali ('those such people' — quei = 'those', cotali = 'such'), followed by a relative clause ch’io accusai di sopra ('that I accused above') where accusai is passato remoto ('I accused'). The final phrase e di lor falli links by coordination: e + di + lor (their) + falli (pl. of fallo, 'faults'), so the whole chunk is the thing you are now able to judge of.
segments.paradiso-6-g93: che is a relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to the faults; son is the contracted poetic form of sono (3rd-person plural present 'are'). cagion is an archaic form of 'cause' and is complemented by di tutti vostri mali ('of all your evils/troubles'), with tutti 'all' and vostri the possessive 'your'.
segments.paradiso-6-g94: L’uno ('the one' or 'one of them') is the subject; al pubblico segno is a prepositional phrase ('to/against the public/official sign') where al = a + il. The direct object i gigli gialli ('the yellow lilies') appears before the verb oppone because of poetic inversion; oppone is 3rd-person singular present of opporre, meaning 'sets/opposes/places against'.
segments.paradiso-6-g95: e l’altro ('and the other one') resumes the contrast; appropria is 3rd-person singular present of appropriare 'claims/appropriates', taking quello ('that one' — the emblem) as direct object. a parte ('to/for a private side') means 'for his own use' or 'to a separate party/interest.'
segments.paradiso-6-g96: sì ch’è is the contracted form of sì che è ('so that it is'); forte a veder is an idiom meaning 'striking/clear to see' (forte here = 'striking/strong'); chi più si falli is a relative clause introduced by chi ('whoever'); si falli is a reflexive form meaning 'errs/misleads himself' (falli is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'to err' or 'to deceive oneself'), with più marking 'most/more'.
segments.paradiso-6-g97: Faccian is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive of fare used here in an optative/jussive sense ('let them do' / 'may they do'); li is the article 'the' and Ghibellin names the faction (Ghibellines). The sentence issues a permissive/imperative wish about their action.
segments.paradiso-6-g98: faccian repeats the optative 'let them do'; lor arte ('their art') means 'their craft' or political cunning (arte here = 'skill/cunning'), and sott’ altro segno ('under another sign') uses the contracted sott' (= sotto) + altro segno to mean 'under another emblem/banner.'
segments.paradiso-6-g99: ché introduces a causal clause ('for/because'); mal segue quello means 'ill/badly follows that one' (mal = 'evil/ill'), and sempre chi ... diparte is a relative clause: chi ('whoever') la giustizia e lui diparte uses diparte (3rd-person present of dipartire) in the sense 'separates' — the two direct objects la giustizia and lui are coordinated by e ('and'), so the phrase means roughly 'whoever separates justice and him.'
segments.paradiso-6-g100: e non l’abbatta is an optative/negative wish ('and may he not overthrow it') where abbattere in subjunctive expresses a hoped-for outcome; esto Carlo novello ('this new Charles') places the demonstrative esto before the name for emphasis, and coi Guelfi suoi ('with his Guelfs') is an instrumental prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment.
segments.paradiso-6-g101: ma ('but') contrasts the previous wish; tema here is a jussive/subjunctive or imperative-like form meaning 'let him fear' / 'may he fear' (or, read as a noun, 'but fear'); de li artigli = 'of the claws/talons'; the relative clause ch’a più alto leon trasser lo vello contains trasser, an archaic/past form meaning 'pulled' (trasse), so 'that have pulled the fleece toward a loftier lion' (a più alto leon = 'to a higher/greater lion'), with lo vello = 'the fleece.'
segments.paradiso-6-g102: Molte fïate (fïate = ‘times/occasions’) is a temporal expression meaning “many times” or “on many occasions”; già adds the sense “already.” Pianser here functions as a past form meaning “lamented” (archaic/poetic past); li figli is the subject “the sons/children.” Per + la colpa del padre means “because of the fault/guilt of the father,” with colpa = guilt/fault and del = di + il.
segments.paradiso-6-g103: E introduces the second clause. Non si creda is an impersonal negative construction with creda in the present subjunctive (third person singular): “one should not believe” or “let it not be believed.” Si is the impersonal passive/subject marker. Che introduces the content clause. Trasmuti is present subjunctive (matching the impersonal), and l’ armi here means “the arms” (armi in a heraldic sense), while per suoi gigli means “for his lilies” (gigli = fleur‑de‑lis; i.e. his descendants or emblem).
segments.paradiso-6-g104: Questa picciola stella si correda: Questa + adjective + noun is straightforward 'this little star'. The clitic si with the verb correda makes the verb reflexive/impersonal here: correda (from corredare) means 'to be accompanied/attended' or 'to furnish with companions' — the si marks that the star 'is accompanied' rather than someone actively equipping it. The following d’ i buoni spirti introduces the agents with the partitive/ possessive di + article (d’i = di + i), 'by/with the good spirits'. The relative clause che son stati attivi uses che = 'who' and the compound son stati (son = present of essere + stati past participle) to mean 'who have been' (perfect sense) + attivi 'active'. Finally perché introduces purpose/result: 'so that' honor and fame might come to them.
segments.paradiso-6-g105: e quando li disiri poggian quivi,: e 'and' + quando 'when'. Here li is the masculine plural article before the noun disiri 'desires' (so 'the desires'). Poggian is an archaic/poetic present subjunctive or present indicative form of poggiare/poggiare (Dante's spelling) meaning 'they rest / are placed' — functionally 'rest there'. Quivi = 'there'. The clause states a temporal condition: 'and when the desires rest there...'.
segments.paradiso-6-g106: sì disvïando,: sì = 'thus/so' here (adverb). Disvïando is the gerund of disviare (note the diaeresis marks the vowel separation typical of Dante), literally 'diverting' or 'leading aside'. The gerund links to the previous clause and explains the manner/cause — 'by thus diverting'.
segments.paradiso-6-g107: pur convien che i raggi del vero amore in sù poggin men vivi.: Pur is an adverb 'still/even/nevertheless' softening what follows. Convien is an impersonal present (convien = 'it is necessary' / 'it must be') and governs a subordinate clause introduced by che + subjunctive. I raggi 'the rays' is the grammatical subject of the subordinate clause; del vero amore 'of true love' is genitive. In sù 'upwards'. Poggin is an archaic/poetic form (here representing the subjunctive of poggiare/poggiare — 'to rest/settle/lie') and so must be read as subjunctive third plural agreeing with raggi ('that the rays ... may lie/settle'). Men modifies vivi: men vivi = 'less vivid' / 'less lively' (i.e. less strongly manifest upward).
segments.paradiso-6-g108: Ma nel commensurar d’i nostri gaggi col merto è parte di nostra letizia,: Ma = 'but'. Nel + infinitive commensurar = 'in (the) measuring/comparing' — Dante often uses an infinitive as a verbal noun. D’i nostri gaggi = 'of our gaggi' (d’ = di + article i); gaggi is an older/regional term here (context indicates 'the young/our offspring' — see note below). Col merto = con + il 'with/by the merit' (i.e. 'by merit'). È parte di nostra letizia = 'there is part of our joy' (è parte = 'is part', di nostra letizia = 'of our joy').
segments.paradiso-6-g109: perché non li vedem minor né maggi.: Perché here gives the reason 'because'. Non li vedem = 'we do not see them' (vedem is Dante's inflected form for 'vediamo' or 'we see'). Minor né maggi = 'less nor greater' — minor = 'smaller/less', né = 'nor'. Maggi in Dante is an archaic comparative (here 'greater'), though the provided sense list did not include this meaning; grammatically it balances minor as the opposite degree. The sentence means: part of our joy springs from judging our young by merit, because we see them neither diminished nor exalted.
segments.paradiso-6-g110: Quindi addolcisce la viva giustizia in noi l’affetto sì,: Quindi = 'hence/therefore'. Addolcisce is the present indicative 'softens'/'sweetens' and its object is l’affetto 'the affection'. Word order is inverted for emphasis: subject (la viva giustizia) follows the verb. Viva qualifies giustizia as 'living' or 'vivid' (in context, 'living/divine justice'). In noi 'in us' specifies the locus. The final sì 'so' intensifies the following result clause.
segments.paradiso-6-g111: che non si puote torcer già mai ad alcuna nequizia.: che introduces the result clause 'that'. Non ... già mai is a negative expression ('no longer / never'), with si puote + infinitive torcer ('it is possible / can be twisted') in a passive/impersonal reflexive construction: non si puote torcer = 'it cannot be twisted'. Ad alcuna nequizia = 'toward any wickedness' (ad + alcuna). The whole clause: 'so that it can never be turned toward any wickedness.'
segments.paradiso-6-g112: Diverse voci fanno dolci note;: Diverse 'different/various' modifies voci 'voices'. Fanno is present 'make' (fanno + direct object). Dolci note 'sweet notes' is a simple noun phrase; note here means 'musical notes' (Dante's language), though the provided sense list for note did not include that musical sense.
segments.paradiso-6-g113: così diversi scanni in nostra vita rendon dolce armonia tra queste rote.: così 'thus/likewise' links the metaphor. Diversi scanni = 'different seats/ranks' (scanni are positions or thrones). In nostra vita 'in our life' gives scope. Rendon is an archaic present plural form of rendere (render/make) meaning 'produce' or 'create'. Dolce armonia 'sweet harmony' is the result produced tra queste rote 'among these wheels' (rote = circles/wheels — the cosmic/astral spheres).

sentences.paradiso-6-s5: As soon as I set out with the Church, it pleased God by grace to inspire in me the lofty task, and I gave myself to it completely; and I entrusted the arms to my Belisarius, to whom the right hand of heaven was so joined that it was a sign I should lay them down.
sentences.paradiso-6-s6: Now my answer is directed at the first question; but its circumstances force me to follow some addition, so that you may see with what reason people move against the sacrosanct standard and who claims it and who opposes it.
sentences.paradiso-6-s7: You see how much virtue has made him worthy of reverence; and it began from the hour when Pallante died to give him a kingdom.
sentences.paradiso-6-s8: You know that he made his dwelling in Alba for three hundred years and more, until the end when the three, three by three, still fought for him.
sentences.paradiso-6-s17: He saw Antandros and Simeonta, from which he set out, and saw there where Hector lies; and afterward, because of Ptolemy, he was grievously shaken.
sentences.paradiso-6-s18: From there he descended, lightning-like, to Juba; from there he turned toward your west, where he heard the Pompeian trumpet.
sentences.paradiso-6-s19: Of what he did with the following bearer, Brutus with Cassius barks in Hell, and Modena and Perugia were sorrowful.
sentences.paradiso-6-s20: Mourn her still, sad Cleopatra, who, fleeing from them ahead, took sudden and black death from the snake.
sentences.paradiso-6-s13: Then, around the time when all heaven willed to restore the world to its calm state, Caesar, by the will of Rome, took him away.
sentences.paradiso-6-s14: And that which he did from the Var as far as the Rhine: he saw the Isère and the Aar and saw the Seine and every valley from which the Rhône is full.
sentences.paradiso-6-s15: What he did afterward, when he left Ravenna and crossed the Rubicon, was of such a sweep that neither tongue nor pen could follow it.
sentences.paradiso-6-s16: He turned his host toward Spain, then toward Durazzo, and struck at Pharsalus so that even the warm Nile felt the grief.
sentences.paradiso-6-s9: And you know that he did — from the wrong of the Sabine women to the sorrow of Lucretia, in seven kings — defeating the neighbouring peoples all around.
sentences.paradiso-6-s10: You know what he accomplished when borne by the illustrious Romans to confront Brennus, to confront Pyrrhus, and to confront the other princes and assemblies; whence Torquatus and Quinctius — he who was named from the neglected cirrus — the Decii and the Fabii had a fame which I would gladly behold.
sentences.paradiso-6-s11: It humbled the pride of the Arabs who, following Hannibal, crossed the alpine rocks — the Po — of which you speak.
sentences.paradiso-6-s12: Under it young men triumphed — Scipio and Pompey; and to that hill under which you were born it seemed bitter.
sentences.paradiso-6-s1: After Constantine turned the eagle toward the course of heaven, which followed the ancient one who took Lavinia, the bird of God remained for a hundred and a hundred years and more at the far end of Europe, close to the mountains from which it first issued; and under the shelter of its sacred feathers it ruled the world there from hand to hand, and, thus changing, it reached upon mine.
sentences.paradiso-6-s2: I was Caesar and I am Justinian, who, by the will of the first love that I feel, drew from within the laws the excessive and the vain.
sentences.paradiso-6-s3: And before I had turned my attention to the work, I believed that there was one nature in Christ, no more, and I was content with such faith; but the blessed Agapitus, who was the supreme shepherd, directed me to sincere faith with his words.
sentences.paradiso-6-s4: I believed him; and that which was in his faith I now see so clearly — as you see — every contradiction both false and true.
sentences.paradiso-6-s21: With him it ran all the way to the red shore; with him the world was set in such peace, that his shrine to Janus was closed.
sentences.paradiso-6-s22: But what the sign that bids me speak had done before and afterwards had brought about for the mortal kingdom that lies subject to him,
sentences.paradiso-6-s23: Now here marvel at what I reply to you: afterwards, with Titus, he ran to take vengeance for the vengeance of the ancient sin.
sentences.paradiso-6-s24: And when the Lombard tooth bit the Holy Church, Charles the Great, under her wings, coming victorious, came to her aid.
sentences.paradiso-6-s33: And within this pearl the light of Romeo shines; whose great and beautiful work was ill-received.
sentences.paradiso-6-s34: But the Provençals who acted against him did not laugh; and therefore he fares badly who harms another's good deeds.
sentences.paradiso-6-s35: Ramondo Beringhiere had four daughters, each one a queen; and Romeo — a humble, pilgrim person — made that happen for them.
sentences.paradiso-6-s36: And then they set their harsh words in motion to demand an explanation from this just man; he allotted them seven and five to make ten, then he departed poor and old; and if the world knew the heart he had while begging his life bit by bit, it praises him much, and would praise him even more.
sentences.paradiso-6-s25: By now you can judge of those people I accused above and of their faults, which are the cause of all your troubles.
sentences.paradiso-6-s26: One sets the yellow lilies against the public emblem, and the other appropriates that emblem for his own use, so that it is plain to see who errs most.
sentences.paradiso-6-s27: Let the Ghibellines act; let them practise their craft under another banner, for ill follows always that one who separates justice from him; and may this new Charles not overthrow it with his Guelfs, but let him fear the claws that have pulled the fleece toward a loftier lion.
sentences.paradiso-6-s28: Many times already the sons have lamented because of the father's fault, and one should not believe that God will change his arms for his lilies!
sentences.paradiso-6-s29: This little star is attended by the good spirits who have been active so that honor and fame may befall them; and when desires rest there, by thus being diverted, still it must be that the rays of true love mount upward less vividly.
sentences.paradiso-6-s30: But in measuring our young by merit there is part of our joy, because we do not see them smaller nor greater.
sentences.paradiso-6-s31: Hence living justice so sweetens affection in us that it can never be twisted toward any wickedness.
sentences.paradiso-6-s32: Different voices make sweet notes; likewise different seats in our life render a sweet harmony among these wheels.
